mnemonics.title: Catch Block Search Order
mnemonics.content: INNER FIRST: Inner to Outer - JVM searches catch blocks from innermost to outermost. First match wins!

mnemonics.title: Finally Execution Rule
mnemonics.content: FINALLY ALWAYS RUNS: Whether exception occurs or not, whether caught or not, finally runs before method exits (unless System.exit() called).

mnemonics.title: Exception Propagation
mnemonics.content: BUBBLE UP: Uncaught exceptions bubble up the call stack like air bubbles in water, seeking a handler at each level.

mnemonics.title: Nested Try Purpose
mnemonics.content: GRANULAR RECOVERY: Use nested try when different code sections need different exception recovery strategies.

acronyms.expansion: Java Virtual Machine - searches for exception handlers from inner to outer try blocks
acronyms.usage: When an exception occurs, JVM starts at the innermost try and looks for matching catch blocks.

acronyms.expansion: Nested Exception STack - think of nested try as stacked trays, must check each level
acronyms.usage: When exception occurs in nested try, check each 'tray' (level) for a handler.

acronyms.expansion: First In Last Out for finally blocks - inner finally executes before outer finally
acronyms.usage: Like stacked plates: remove top (inner finally) first, then bottom (outer finally).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a Russian nesting doll (Matryoshka). Each doll (try block) can either handle the exception or pass it to the larger doll (outer try). Start checking from the smallest doll.
Think of exception as water pouring down. First, it tries to fill the innermost container (inner catch). If too big, it overflows to the next container (outer catch), and so on.
Stack of papers on a desk. When you leave (method exit), you remove the top paper first (inner finally), then the next (outer finally). Reverse order of placement.
Like a key-lock system. The JVM tries each catch key in the innermost lock first. First key that fits opens the door (handles exception).

analogies.analogy: Like a security system with multiple layers. Inner try is like a room detector - handles specific issues in that room. Outer try is like the building alarm - handles issues that pass through or affect the whole building.
Like reporting problems up a corporate hierarchy. First, your immediate supervisor (inner catch) handles it. If they can't, it goes to their manager (outer catch), then up the chain.
Like a 'cleanup crew' that always reports after any event - whether the event succeeded or failed. Each level of nesting has its own cleanup crew that reports in order.
Like having both a firewall and antivirus. Inner catch (firewall) handles network issues first. If something gets through, outer catch (antivirus) handles system-level issues.
